steps.title: Find the key signal
steps.content: The phrase “for example” indicates the second clause exemplifies the claim about “many are still well ______”.

steps.title: Apply the colon rule
steps.content:
Identify the structure: "many are still well [blank]" is an independent clause (IC). What follows — "Malika Ouattara, for example, is a celebrated slam poet..." — is also an IC that exemplifies the claim. Rule: a colon after an IC introduces an example or elaboration.
  • Choice A — "known,": "many are still well known, Malika Ouattara..." — joins two ICs with only a comma: comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — "known:": "many are still well known: Malika Ouattara..." — colon after a complete IC introduces the example. ✓
  • Choice C — "known and": "many are still well known and Malika Ouattara..." — "and" would need a comma before it and doesn't logically connect the two ICs here. ✗
  • Choice D — "known": "many are still well known Malika Ouattara..." — no punctuation between two ICs creates a run-on. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
